content.text: Radar Governance Cadence and Publication Process

A technology radar is only useful if it is kept current and engineers trust its evaluation process. Both require explicit governance: a defined cadence for reviewing blips, a transparent process for proposing changes, and a repeatable publication workflow that produces a credible artefact on a predictable schedule.

The cadence serves two conflicting needs: stability (teams need to rely on evaluated positions without them shifting weekly) and currency (the Claude ecosystem moves fast, and a stale radar gives wrong guidance). Biannual published editions with continuous background proposals balance these.
Between formal editions, the following activities run continuously to ensure the radar stays current:
Renovate/Dependabot PRs for Anthropic SDK patch, minor, and major releases. Architecture team reviews minor+ changes within the week.
A daily job checks the Anthropic model catalog API for new or removed models. Alerts fire to the architecture team Slack channel.
GitHub Security Advisories for anthropic, @anthropic-ai/sdk, and all direct dependencies. CVEs trigger emergency Hold placement within 48 hours.
Engineers submit blip proposals via RFC at any time. Proposals accumulate in a backlog and are batched for the next quarterly review.
A spreadsheet or internal tool tracks every model's and SDK version's known end-of-support date, with owner, migration status, and ETA.
Each quarter, the architecture board convenes a focused 90-minute session to:
Review accumulated blip proposals from the past quarter — approve, reject, or request more evidence
Act on any deprecation notices received since the last review — move affected blips to Assess or Hold
Review open pilot results — advance blips from Assess to Trial where pilots succeeded
Identify any blips that have been in a ring too long without evidence of evaluation — schedule or close them
Quarterly reviews do not produce a new published edition — they update an internal working state that feeds into the next biannual edition.
Biannual Full Editions (H1 — April, H2 — October)

Twice per year, all pending movements are consolidated, blip text is finalized, the architecture board votes, and a new edition is published. The biannual schedule gives teams a predictable window for planning technology adoption and gives authors enough time to write quality blip rationale.
Some events require immediate action without waiting for the next quarterly or biannual slot:
CVE in Anthropic SDK or major dependency
Emergency Hold; security patch PR opened immediately
Anthropic removes a model from the API
Emergency Hold for all applications using that model; migration alert to owners
Anthropic announces a future end-of-support date
Move affected model to Assess; add to deprecation tracker; assign migration owner
Critical post-mortem implicates a Hold violation
Hold placement for violated technology; RFC required for exception if continued use is needed

The RFC process ensures that blip movement proposals have structured evidence and are not driven by individual preferences. Any engineer can submit a proposal at any time; the architecture board reviews it at the next quarterly.

# RFC-XXXX: [Technology Name] — Proposed Ring: [Ring]

**Author:** @engineer-name
**Date:** YYYY-MM-DD
**Current Ring:** [current ring or NEW]
**Proposed Ring:** [target ring]
**Quadrant:** Tooling & SDKs | Integrations & Patterns

## Summary
One or two sentences describing what is being proposed and why.

## Evidence

### For Promotion (Assess→Trial or Trial→Adopt)
- PoC or pilot results: [link to repo/document]
- Teams/use-cases validated: [list]
- Eval suite results: [pass rate, sample size]
- Known limitations or caveats:

### For Demotion to Hold
- Security advisory or incident: [link/ID]
- Post-mortem reference: [link/ID]
- Risk description:
- Compensating controls (if any):

## Proposed Ring Rationale
Two to four paragraphs explaining why the proposed ring is the right assignment.
Reference the evidence above. Acknowledge counterarguments.

## Impact
- Teams currently using this technology: [list]
- Migration required? [Yes/No] If yes, timeline and owner:
- ADRs or RFCs that reference this technology: [links]

## Watch Conditions (for Hold or Assess)
What observable event would trigger re-evaluation?
Include a target review date if possible.

## Alternatives Considered
List any alternative technologies considered and why they were not proposed.
The architecture board evaluates proposals against these criteria:
Is the evidence specific and verifiable (PoC results, incident numbers, CVE IDs) or vague ('seems better')? Vague evidence → request more evidence before voting.
Does the evidence come from use cases relevant to your organization's Claude deployments, or just industry benchmarks?
Does the proposal accurately list all current users and migration requirements? Incomplete impact = RFC sent back for revision.
For Hold/Assess placements, is the watch condition observable and time-bound? Vague watch conditions erode radar trust over time.
Has the proposer disclosed any vendor relationship or personal stake in the technology's adoption?

attrs.alt: Radar Publication Process: From Data Freeze to Distribution

content.text: The radar diagram should be generated from code, not maintained as a hand-edited image. Treat the blip register as source data and generate both the interactive diagram and the changelog automatically:

Each published edition should be tagged in git so the full history of radar positions is preserved. This enables retrospective accuracy assessment — were the ring assignments correct? — and provides audit evidence for compliance questions:

# Tag the edition in git after publishing
git tag -a radar-2025-H2 -m "Tech Radar Edition 2025-H2 — 6 blip movements"

# Package the edition data for archiving
cp dist/radar-data.json editions/2025-H2.json
git add editions/2025-H2.json
git commit -m "archive: radar edition 2025-H2"
git push origin main --tags

# The editions/ directory provides:
# - editions/2025-H1.json
# - editions/2025-H2.json
# - editions/2026-H1.json  (future)
# Useful for: accuracy retrospectives, compliance audits, diffing between editions
Publication without communication is invisible. Use a multi-channel communication plan that reaches engineers where they work:
Summary of key movements + link to radar site
In-depth changelog with blip rationale highlights
Walk through all movements; Q&A on controversial blips
Link to current radar as the technology reference
Radar status check field; links to current edition
Drive cadence; chair board reviews; own publication process
Review and vote on proposals; author blip entries for their domain
Write blip text for new or moved entries; gather evidence
Submit RFC with evidence; respond to board questions
Ensure security-motivated Hold placements happen on time

The radar owner is accountable for the cadence but not for all decisions. The architecture board makes ring assignments by majority vote. This separation prevents the radar from becoming one person's opinion dressed up as process.
Track these metrics to assess whether the governance process is producing value:
Median days from RFC submission to board decision. Target: < 90 days. Long backlogs indicate insufficient review bandwidth.
Number of production deployments found using Hold technologies per quarter. Target: 0. Non-zero indicates radar is not being consulted.
Six months after each edition, review each movement: was the ring assignment vindicated? Track percentage of blip movements that proved correct.
Hours from CVE publication to Hold placement on the radar. Target: < 48h.
Percentage of engineers who opened the edition changelog within 30 days of publication. Indicates communication plan effectiveness.
The governance cadence described above only scales if the mechanics are automated. Manual tracking in a spreadsheet works for a team of five; a team of fifty needs tooling.
Manage the RFC backlog as GitHub Issues with standardised labels, so the architecture board can triage proposals without a separate meeting tracker:

# .github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/blip-proposal.yml
name: Tech Radar Blip Proposal
description: Propose adding or moving a blip on the Claude ecosystem radar
labels: ["radar-proposal", "needs-review"]
assignees: ["architecture-board"]
body:
  - type: markdown
    attributes:
      value: "Fill this template fully. Incomplete proposals will be returned."

  - type: input
    id: technology
    attributes:
      label: Technology Name
      placeholder: "e.g. LangChain, AWS Bedrock, claude-haiku-4-5-20251001"
    validations:
      required: true

  - type: dropdown
    id: proposed_ring
    attributes:
      label: Proposed Ring
      options: ["adopt", "trial", "assess", "hold"]
    validations:
      required: true

  - type: dropdown
    id: current_ring
    attributes:
      label: Current Ring (or NEW if not yet on radar)
      options: ["NEW", "adopt", "trial", "assess", "hold"]
    validations:
      required: true

  - type: dropdown
    id: quadrant
    attributes:
      label: Quadrant
      options: ["Tooling & SDKs", "Integrations & Patterns"]
    validations:
      required: true

  - type: textarea
    id: evidence
    attributes:
      label: Evidence
      description: >
        For promotions: link to PoC repo, pilot results, eval suite output.
        For demotions: incident number, CVE ID, post-mortem link.
    validations:
      required: true

  - type: textarea
    id: rationale
    attributes:
      label: Ring Rationale
      description: Why is this ring the right assignment? Acknowledge counterarguments.
    validations:
      required: true

  - type: textarea
    id: impact
    attributes:
      label: Impact and Migration
      description: Which teams use this? Is migration required? Who owns it?
    validations:
      required: true

  - type: textarea
    id: watch_conditions
    attributes:
      label: Watch Conditions (for Hold/Assess)
      description: What observable event triggers re-evaluation?
A GitHub Actions workflow can check that every RFC and ADR referencing a Claude ecosystem technology is consistent with the current radar ring:

# .github/workflows/radar-compliance.yml
name: Radar Compliance Check
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'docs/rfcs/**'
      - 'docs/adrs/**'

jobs:
  check:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install deps
        run: npm ci

      - name: Check radar compliance
        run: |
          # Extract PR description and changed files
          PR_BODY="${{ github.event.pull_request.body }}"
          CHANGED_FILES=$(git diff --name-only origin/main...HEAD)

          # Run compliance checker against radar-data.json
          node scripts/check-radar-compliance.mjs             --body "$PR_BODY"             --files $CHANGED_FILES             --radar dist/radar-data.json             --fail-on hold
Proposals sit for 6+ months; engineers shadow-adopt Assess technologies
Delegate minor promotions (Assess→Trial) to tech leads; board only votes on major moves
Ring decisions are made informally in Slack; no audit trail
Require a GitHub Issue for every proposal; close verbal decisions won't count
Engineers don't know the radar changed; continue using deprecated tools
Send edition email to all engineers within 24 hours of publication, always
'We prefer X' is not an evidence-based ring placement
Enforce RFC template; reviewer rejects without quantitative or sourced evidence
Internal tools that compete with external ones placed in Hold
Hold must cite specific technical or security evidence; competitive reasons are not valid
ADRs reference tools without checking radar status; radar is ignored in practice
Add a radar-status required field to the ADR template; link to current edition
Many teams want a radar but are overwhelmed by starting with hundreds of blips. The most effective approach is to start small and build momentum:
Cover only the technologies your team currently uses or is actively evaluating. An authoritative small radar beats an exhaustive inaccurate one.
Seed Adopt with what you actually run in production.
If your team has been using the Anthropic Python SDK in production for months, it belongs in Adopt immediately — no evaluation needed.
Put everything else in Assess for the first edition.
It is honest: you haven't formally evaluated them. They will move to their correct rings over the following two editions.
Announce it as 'Edition 1 — Work in Progress'.
Explicit humility about incompleteness is better than pretending you've evaluated everything. Teams trust honest radars more than authoritative-sounding ones.
Set your first quarterly review date before publishing.
The publication announcement should include the next review date. This signals that the radar is a living process, not a one-time effort.

Radar governance cadence converts a point-in-time artefact into a living decision-support system. The biannual publication cycle provides stability; continuous monitoring, quarterly reviews, and an always-open RFC process provide currency. Clear roles, a structured proposal template, and measurable effectiveness metrics prevent the radar from degenerating into a stale document that engineers have learned to ignore.
Publish two editions per year (H1 in April, H2 in October) with a 4-week production process from data freeze to distribution
Run quarterly reviews to process accumulated proposals and act on deprecation notices
Reserve emergency slots for security advisories and model removals — respond within 48 hours
Use a structured RFC template for all blip proposals so decisions are evidence-driven, not opinion-driven
Generate the radar diagram from code using the blip register as source data — never maintain it as a hand-edited image
Tag each edition in git and archive the blip data for retrospective accuracy assessment and compliance auditing
